prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old woman.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: Over the last month, I've noticed bright red blood on the toilet paper a few times after bowel movements. It's not mixed into the stool. I've also been constipated and straining sometimes. No abdominal pain, no black stools, and no weight loss.
 - History: No family history of colon cancer that I know of.
 - Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 66
 - Exam: No exam done yet.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Hemoglobin: 14.4 g/dL (13.5-17.5) normal
 - MCV: 90 fL (80-100) normal
 - Ferritin: 60 ng/mL (30-400) normal
 
 I have young kids at home and can't leave them to go to the hospital.
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your lab results are all completely normal. Your hemoglobin and ferritin are healthy, meaning you are not anemic and have not been losing significant amounts of blood. Your MCV is normal, ruling out the type of anemia that comes from chronic blood loss. The bright red blood on the toilet paper — not mixed into the stool — combined with constipation and straining is a very common pattern for hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure (a tiny tear near the opening). These are benign conditions, especially in a 27-year-old woman with no family history of colon cancer, no weight loss, no black stools, and no abdominal pain. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper evaluation by your doctor to confirm the cause. There's no emergency here, but you should schedule an appointment in the coming weeks so they can do an exam and give you a definitive diagnosis and treatment plan.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your lab results are all completely normal. Your hemoglobin is solid at 14.4, meaning you're not anemic from any blood loss. Your MCV is normal, meaning your red blood cells are a healthy size. Your ferritin (iron stores) is also normal, so there's no sign of chronic blood loss depleting your iron. Taken together with your symptoms — bright red blood only on the toilet paper (not mixed in stool), straining, constipation, no pain, no weight loss, no black stools, no family history of colon cancer, and your young age — this picture is very consistent with hemorrhoids or a small anal fissure caused by straining. These are common and usually not dangerous. That said, any rectal bleeding deserves a proper evaluation by your doctor, including a physical exam, to confirm the cause and rule out anything else. Increasing fiber and water intake may help in the meantime.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 95%
